front: What is a null hypothesis (H₀) in statistical testing?
back: The null hypothesis is a default statement that there is no relationship, no effect, or no difference between groups. It is the hypothesis that the statistical test aims to gather evidence against.

front: What is a p-value, and how is it interpreted?
back: A p-value is the probability of obtaining test results at least as extreme as the observed results, assuming that the null hypothesis is true. A small p-value (typically ≤ 0.05) indicates strong evidence against the null hypothesis, leading to its rejection.

front: What is the difference between a Type I error and a Type II error?
back: A Type I error (false positive) occurs when the null hypothesis is incorrectly rejected when it is true. A Type II error (false negative) occurs when the null hypothesis is not rejected when it is actually false.

front: What is statistical power, and what factors influence it?
back: Statistical power is the probability that a test will correctly reject a false null hypothesis (i.e., avoid a Type II error). It is influenced by the significance level (α), the sample size (n), and the effect size. Larger samples and larger effect sizes increase power.

front: What is the purpose of A/B testing in the context of machine learning and data science?
back: A/B testing is a randomized controlled experiment used to compare two versions (A and B) of a single variable, typically a web page, feature, or model. Its purpose is to determine which version performs better on a specific metric (e.g., click-through rate, conversion rate) using statistical hypothesis testing.
